---
title: "GOV 51: PSET 02"
author: "Daniel Cabrera"
format: pdf
editor: visual
---

# GOV 51: PSET 02

## Daniel Cabrera

GitHub Repository Link: <https://github.com/daniel-e-cabrera/gov51-ps2.git>

### 1.1.1 

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
gay <- read_csv("data/raw/gay.csv")
```

### Q1.1

```{r}
#checking out the raw data
View(gay)
nrow(gay)
length(unique(gay$study))
unique(gay$treatment)

```

In our gay data set, there are a total of 69592 observations. Additionally, there are 2 unique values for our variable study. Lastly, the treatment conditions for our treatment variable are: "No Contact", "Recycling Script by Gay Canvasser", "Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser", "Recycling Script by Straight Canvasser", and "Same-Sex Marriage Script by Straight Canvasser".

### Q1.2

```{r}
#filtering the data
gay_s1_w1 <- gay |>
  filter(study == "1",
         wave == "1")
View(gay_s1_w1)

#looking at filtered data mean
gay_s1_w1 |>
  group_by(treatment) |>
  summarise(mean_ssm = mean(ssm))

```

The treatment group with the highest average support for gay individuals is the "Recycling Script by Gay Canvasser" treatment group. They have a mean same-sex marriage attitude score of 3.13 (scored from 1-5 with 1 being the least amount of support for gay individuals and 5 being the most support for gay individuals).

### Q1.3

```{r}
gay_s1_w1 |>
  group_by(treatment) |>
  summarise(sd_ssm = sd(ssm))

```

The standard deviations of each treatment group are roughly similar. Each one is between 1.66 and 1.7. Overall, our similar standard deviations tell us that people's responses are about equally dispersed around their treatment group's mean. Additionally, this is important, specfically in a randomized experiment because our differences in spread can be interpreted as treatment effects. Specifically, it tells us that treatments aren't really making people very polarized. In other words, outcome variability is nearly identical (SDs 1.66–1.70 on a 1–5 scale), suggesting the treatments do not meaningfully affect dispersion and that differences in means are not confounded by differences in variance.

### Q1.4

```{r}
#summary data frame
summary_gay_s1_w1 <- gay_s1_w1 |>
  group_by(treatment) |>
  summarise(mean_ssm = mean(ssm))

#bar plot
bar_plot <- ggplot(summary_gay_s1_w1, aes(x = treatment, y = mean_ssm)) +
  geom_col(fill = "dodgerblue", width = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Mean Support for Same-Sex Marriage by Treatment Condition",
       subtitle = "Randomized Study",
       x = "Treatment Condition",
       y = "Mean SSM Score (1–5 Scale)") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
print(bar_plot)

#saving to output folder
ggsave("output/bar_plot.png", width = 10, height = 6)
```

### Q1.5

```{r}
comparison_gay_s1_w1 <- gay_s1_w1 |>
  filter(treatment %in% c("Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser", "No Contact")) |>
  group_by(treatment) |>
  summarise(mean_ssm = mean(ssm), sd_ssm = sd(ssm))
print(comparison_gay_s1_w1)
  
```

```{r}
(3.025195 - 3.042764) / sqrt((1.666099^2 + 1.680540^2)/2)
```

Yes, these groups look similar at baseline. With a standardized difference in means of −0.01, which is very close to 0, we can conclude that the two treatment/control groups are essentially balanced on this baseline measure—there is virtually no meaningful difference in their average baseline scores. This suggests that any differences we observe later in ssm scores are unlikely to be driven by pre-treatment differences.
